title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Marine
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: CISA flags critical API flaws in iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals — the VSAT box nearly every vessel depends on
items.content_text: On 2 July CISA warned (ICSA-26-183-01) that two high-severity API flaws in ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals let unauthenticated attackers on the network pull sensitive device data and remotely reboot the unit. For maritime operators that is a direct hit on the box most vessels, rigs and remote terminals depend on for connectivity: iDirect provides the VSAT backhaul linking ship to shore. CVE-2026-38059 exposes serial numbers, device and key identifiers and firmware over unauthenticated REST endpoints; CVE-2026-38057 lets a malicious web page an authenticated admin visits trigger a reboot, and repeated abuse sustains a denial of the satellite link — cutting navigation data, reporting and safety comms at sea. iDirect fixed it in software 4.5.2.2; terminals on 4.5.2.1 or earlier need updating.

items.title: GPS/GNSS interference keeps spoofing vessels off course — roughly 1,000 disruptions a day
items.content_text: GNSS interference has become a routine hazard rather than a wartime exception, with reporting citing on the order of 1,000 spoofing or jamming events daily affecting tens of thousands of vessels. Incidents range from ships shown hundreds of miles from their true position to AIS tracks jumping erratically at impossible speeds — and even a scheduled U.S. Navy test off Long Beach in January knocked out GPS for seven inbound vessels within an hour. The lesson for bridge teams is to treat GNSS as spoofable: cross-check position with radar, terrestrial aids and dead reckoning, and train crews to recognize and report anomalies.

items.title: Counterfeit malware-laced USB drives breached classified military networks — a warning for vessel OT
items.content_text: Reporting describes counterfeit USB drives carrying China-linked malware that bypassed procurement channels and infected more than 50 computers, including closed classified networks, during 2024 disaster-relief operations — with similar tainted devices still sold through mainstream online retailers. For maritime OT the parallel is direct: engine, cargo and navigation systems are frequently updated by USB in air-gapped or lightly connected environments, making removable media one of the few reliable ways onto a ship's control network. Controlling and scanning USB media is a low-cost, high-value control.

items.title: FortiBleed credential theft now feeds ransomware — ports and shipping lines run on the same firewalls
items.content_text: SOCRadar reported on 2 July that FortiBleed — a sniffer that harvested 110M+ credentials from roughly 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls since mid-June — is now driving INC Ransom and Lynx intrusions. Ports, terminals and shipping lines lean heavily on Fortinet for remote access to terminal operating systems, crane and gate controls and vessel-to-shore links. Because credentials leaked rather than a bug being exploited, no patch helps: an attacker signs in as a legitimate administrator. Assume any exposed FortiGate touching port or vessel OT is compromised and rotate credentials now.

items.title: China-linked CL-STA-1062 targets regional critical infrastructure with TinyRCT — ports sit in the blast radius
items.content_text: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 reported in late June that a Chinese-speaking group, CL-STA-1062, compromised electricity, water, government and military organizations across Southeast Asia using a new backdoor, TinyRCT, built for stealthy persistence and exfiltration. Ports and terminals — concentrations of logistics, government and OT systems astride strategic chokepoints — fit squarely in this pattern of nation-state pre-positioning. The defensive emphasis is the same: hunt for quiet footholds in terminal IT and prove they cannot pivot into the operational systems that move cargo and vessels.

items.title: Citrix NetScaler flaws echo CitrixBleed — the remote-access edge at ports and shipping offices
items.content_text: Citrix has patched six vulnerabilities in NetScaler ADC and Gateway (CVSS 6.9–8.8), including CVE-2026-8451, a SAML-related memory-disclosure bug researchers compare to the severe 2023 CitrixBleed. NetScaler fronts remote access for many port authorities, terminal operators and shipping lines, so a memory-disclosure flaw that leaks session material is a plausible path to hijacked administrator access. There is no confirmed in-the-wild exploitation yet, but one fix requires a specific manual configuration change in addition to the patch — miss it and the box stays exposed.

items.title: FCC votes to toughen submarine-cable security rules — the arteries under global shipping lanes
items.content_text: On 25 June the FCC voted unanimously to modernize the rules governing submarine cables that carry the vast majority of transoceanic internet, financial and data traffic, adding mandatory cybersecurity and physical-security risk-management plans for submarine line terminal equipment operators and excluding entities tied to designated foreign adversaries. The same order tightened cyber-hygiene requirements for emergency-alert participants. For the maritime sector it signals rising regulatory attention to the undersea and coastal communications infrastructure that ports, vessels and offshore operations quietly depend on.

items.title: USCG Marine Transportation System cyber rule is in force — assessments and reporting are now table stakes
items.content_text: The U.S. Coast Guard's Cybersecurity in the Marine Transportation System rule, effective since mid-2025, requires U.S.-flagged vessels and regulated waterfront and offshore facilities to designate a Cybersecurity Officer, run annual assessments, report incidents to the National Response Center, and complete crew cybersecurity training — a deadline that passed in January. Operators should treat this as an operating baseline, not a project: keep assessments current, exercise the reporting path, and align vessel and facility security plans with the rule as enforcement matures.
